title: Chapter 12: The Count
content: The badge felt like a talisman.

Maya turned it over in her fingers for the third time that morning — REYES, M., AV TECHNICIAN, CONTRACTOR — laminated plastic with a magnetic stripe and a headshot where she'd managed to look competent instead of terrified. She clipped it to her lanyard and walked into Aeterna's third floor like she'd been doing it for years.

Second day. Official access. A desk near the south window — smart glass, currently transparent — and a rolling cart of cable testers, adapters, and diagnostic tools. Also: seven of Chloe's micro-transmitters, each one nestled inside a modified HDMI adapter, waiting to be deployed across the building's AV infrastructure like seeds scattered in hostile soil.

She'd passed. Marcus Webb had approved her with that thin, satisfied smile of a man who believed he'd made a smart hire. The background check — Chloe's fabricated identity layered over the real skeleton of Maya's credentials — had come back clean. Of course it had. Chloe had built the legend like an architect building a load-bearing wall: Maya Reyes, San Jose City College audio technology certificate, three years freelance AV work, impeccable references who would enthusiastically confirm every line of the resume because those references were Chloe on three different VoIP lines.
So here she was. Inside.

The morning was a slow, methodical deployment. Server room first — she'd filed a ticket for audio routing diagnostics on the third-floor rack, and the IT lead had waved her in without looking up from his monitors. She swapped two adapters on the rack's AV bridge, each one clicking into place with the satisfying precision of a round chambering. Conference Room 7-C. The executive presentation suite on four. The all-hands meeting space on two, where she spent forty-five minutes under the pretense of tracing a phantom ground loop while sliding a transmitter into the cable run behind the credenza. Each one pinged Chloe's receiver in the van parked two blocks east — six for six, green lights across the board.

Maya moved through Aeterna like she belonged. That was the trick. Not hiding — hiding drew the eye. Belonging. Walking with purpose, keeping her tools visible, nodding at the people she passed with the easy authority of someone who had every reason to be there. She asked the facilities manager where the AV closet for the west corridor was located, and he'd pointed without hesitation. That corridor led to the restricted elevator bank — the one that required a biometric scan and descended to sublevels that weren't on any floor directory Maya could find.

She planted the seventh transmitter in the AV closet's cable trunk, right where the feeds from the corridor security cameras converged. Chloe would have eyes on every person who entered or left that elevator.
Six transmitters down. One to go.
Maya was under Conference Room 5-B's table, running her hands along the bundled HDMI and power cables, when she heard the footsteps.

They were measured. Even. The click of leather on polished concrete — not the rubber-soled shuffle of the Aeterna staff, not the quick cadence of someone with somewhere to be. These were footsteps that belonged to someone who owned the ground they walked on.
They stopped.
Maya looked up.

He filled the doorway. Not physically — the door was wide enough for two — but something about his presence compressed the space, bent the light around him like a gravitational event. He was tall. Six-two, maybe six-three, with the kind of lean, architectural frame that made his black turtleneck look tailored rather than casual. A vintage watch on his left wrist — she caught the glint of rose gold, the patina of age. His face—
His face was the problem.

It was symmetrical. Perfectly symmetrical, the way faces aren't supposed to be. High cheekbones that cast precise shadows, a jawline that could have been machined, lips that sat in a neutral line with the exact fullness on both sides. Every proportion fell into the golden ratio, and the effect was not beauty — it was wrong. It was a Renaissance painting rendered in skin, technically flawless and deeply, viscerally unsettling. The uncanny valley made architecture.
His eyes found her under the table.

They were dark. Not brown-dark — dark dark, like pupils that had swallowed the iris entirely. Deep-set beneath a strong brow. And they were empty. Not cold, not calculating — empty, the way a house is empty after the family has moved out and taken everything, even the light bulbs.
"You're the new audio technician."

Not a question. A statement of fact, delivered in a voice like old velvet — smooth, refined, accented in a way she couldn't place. Eastern European, maybe, but flattened by decades or centuries of practice into something geographically impossible to pin down.
Maya kept her hands steady on the cables. "Yes, Mr. Vlad. Maya Reyes."
He didn't blink. She counted — one second, two, three — and his eyes didn't flicker. No moisture, no micro-movements. Just that steady, bottomless gaze.
"I hear audio equipment is very sensitive," he said. "It picks up things people don't intend to share."

There it was. The test. Delivered without emphasis, without a change in posture, without the telltale lean forward that indicated someone pressing an advantage. He was perfectly still — the kind of still that living things never achieved, because living things breathed, shifted, pulsed.
Maya felt for the cable adapter in her pocket — the last transmitter, still undeployed. The hard edges of the casing pressed against her palm like a talon. She kept her face neutral, her voice level.
"That's why you hire professionals, sir. We know when to listen and when to..." She let the pause breathe for exactly one beat. "...edit."

Vlad's mouth twitched. Not a smile — she wanted to be clear about that. A smile involves the eyes, the cheeks, a constellation of micro-expressions that signal warmth or amusement. This was a twitch. A mechanical acknowledgment that something had been said that warranted a response. The ghost of a smile, if the ghost had never been alive.
"Indeed." He turned his head slightly — a fraction of a degree — toward the corridor behind him. "Welcome to Aeterna, Ms. Reyes. I look forward to hearing what you discover."
He left. The footsteps resumed — that same measured, owning cadence — and receded down the corridor. The air in the conference room shifted, as if a pressure system had moved through.
Maya's hands were shaking under the table.

She pressed her palms flat against the cool carpet and breathed. In through the nose, out through the mouth. The sunglasses camera — Chloe's modification, a pinhole lens built into the upper left corner of the frame — had been recording the entire time. Maya hadn't looked directly at Vlad through the lens, but the peripheral capture would be enough.
She finished cabling the conference room on autopilot. Her hands steadied eventually. Her heartbeat didn't.
Chloe called at 7:14 PM, after Maya was back in the safe house with the lights on and every lock engaged.
"Tell me about the meeting," Chloe said.
"I already did. Over text."
"I want to hear your voice when you say it. Your voice tells me things your thumbs don't."
Maya sat on the edge of the bed, her shoes still on. "He knew I was there. He came specifically. That wasn't accidental — he wanted to see me, to look at me. It was like being scanned."
"Okay. Now I'm going to show you something, and I need you to not scream."
Chloe screen-shared. Two images side by side.

The left image was a still frame from the sunglasses camera — Vlad in the conference room doorway, that perfect face angled slightly toward Maya. The corridor behind him was lit by standard overhead fluorescents, the kind that cast light from multiple angles and eliminate deep shadows. The other objects in the frame — the doorframe, a fire extinguisher, a potted plant — all had soft, diffuse shadows beneath them.
Vlad had none.

Not a reduced shadow. Not a faint shadow. None. The fluorescent light fell on his body and did not interact with it. His silhouette against the wall behind him was crisp and shadowless, as if he'd been digitally composited into the scene.
Maya stared. "That's—"
"The camera didn't glitch. I checked the raw data. The luminance values are consistent across every frame. He literally cast no shadow, Maya."

The right image was a thermal overlay of the same frame. Chloe had run it through a processing pipeline that estimated body temperature from the infrared data captured by the camera's secondary sensor. The color mapping showed the conference room at approximately 22°C — standard office temperature. The walls, the doorframe, the potted plant — all 22°C, give or take.
Vlad was 14°C.

Room temperature. Not body temperature. Room temperature, as if the heat that should have been radiating from a living body — the metabolic furnace of a hundred trillion cells burning glucose — simply did not exist.
"Fourteen degrees," Maya said, her voice barely above a whisper.
"A corpse loses about 1.5 degrees per hour in ambient room temperature," Chloe said, her own voice tight and controlled. "To hit 14°C, a body would need to be dead for roughly five to six hours. Or—"
"Or it was never alive."
Silence on the line. Then Chloe exhaled. "We need more data."
"Chloe."
"Yeah."
"He knows. You heard what he said. 'I look forward to hearing what you discover.' That wasn't a greeting. That was a threat."
"Or a test. Or a provocation. Or all three." A pause. "He's playing with us, Maya. He wanted you to know he sees you."
The podcast had fifty thousand plays by midnight.

Maya's phone buzzed with notifications she'd stopped checking — Twitter mentions, DMs, a Slack thread from an audio engineering group she hadn't posted in since 2024. The episode — Episode 3: "The Wellness Retreat" — had hit a nerve she hadn't fully anticipated. She'd told Lucy Chen's story with care, with documentation, with the same forensic attention she'd once brought to her mixing boards. She'd laid out the timeline: the Aeterna retreat invitation, the blood work, the IV drip, the disappearance. She'd played the voicemail Lucy had left on her mother's phone two days before she vanished — confused, slurred, apologizing for something she couldn't articulate.
The internet listened.

#FindLucy trended on Twitter by morning. The thread came alive with stories — women who'd been to Aeterna retreats, women who'd left one-star Google reviews that were mysteriously deleted, women who'd filed police reports that went nowhere. One post went viral within hours: *I went to an Aeterna retreat and woke up with IV marks I couldn't explain. Three on my left arm. I have no memory of them being inserted.* Another: *They took so much blood I fainted and they told me it was a "healing reaction." I should have gone to the hospital. I should have gone to the police. I was too ashamed.*
The internet was doing what the police wouldn't. It was listening.

Marcus Webb's office was on the fourth floor, corner position with the smart glass set to maximum opacity. Vlad stood by the window — or rather, by the wall of opaque glass that replaced the window — his hands clasped behind his back, watching nothing.

"The podcast is a problem," Marcus said. He stood behind his desk, a tablet in hand, scrolling through metrics. "Fifty thousand overnight. Trending on Twitter. Three major news outlets have reached out for comment."
"How many voices?" Vlad asked.
"Excuse me?"
"The women sharing stories. How many?"
Marcus checked. "Forty-seven distinct accounts in the last twelve hours. Probably more by—"
"She is resourceful." Vlad turned from the glass wall. His face, in the filtered light of the office, looked like carved marble — alabaster with faint blue undertones. "Who is the host?"
"Maya Reyes." Marcus hesitated. Vlad waited. The silence had weight. "Your new AV technician."

Vlad's expression did not change. Not a single muscle moved in his face. The stillness was absolute — more disturbing than anger, more threatening than any display of violence. A snake doesn't need to rear back to be dangerous. It just needs to be still.
"How interesting," Vlad said.

He left Marcus standing in the office, the tablet still in his hands, and walked into the corridor — the same corridor where, beneath the overhead fluorescents, his silhouette moved across the wall without a shadow.
Chloe intercepted the PR response at 3:47 PM.

Whitby Communications — the crisis management firm Aeterna retained for situations exactly like this — had pushed a statement to all major outlets within a four-hour window. Classic containment protocol: acknowledge, minimize, redirect.

*Aeterna is aware of irresponsible social media speculation regarding our wellness programs. These programs are voluntary, evidence-based, and transformative, supported by decades of clinical research and thousands of positive participant outcomes. We encourage anyone with concerns to contact us directly through legitimate channels rather than relying on unsubstantiated online narratives.*
Maya read it twice. "Gaslighting."
"Textbook," Chloe confirmed. "They're framing the survivors as irresponsible speculators and positioning themselves as the reasonable adults. 'Legitimate channels' — meaning channels they control."
"It's exactly what I predicted."

"It is. And it would have worked two years ago. Maybe even six months ago." Chloe's voice carried a note of grim satisfaction. "But the internet doesn't work that way anymore. Forty-seven women, Maya. That's not speculation — that's a pattern. And patterns don't go back in the box."
"Unless the box has really good lawyers."
"Whitby's good. But they're not gods." A pause. "And neither is he."
Maya wasn't so sure about that.

She left Aeterna at 6:22 PM, the last transmitter deployed — the conference room under 5-B, where Vlad had stood in the doorway and looked through her like she was glass. Her badge got her through the third-floor security checkpoint. Her toolbox passed the front desk inspection. Her face, she hoped, passed the scrutiny of every camera she walked past.

The parking lot was half-empty. The October sky was bleeding orange and purple at the horizon, the sun sinking behind the hills. Maya's Civic sat in the far corner where she'd left it, baking in the last of the daylight.
Her phone buzzed.
Chloe. Two messages, sent seconds apart.
**Vlad is moving. He just called an all-hands security meeting.**
**They're locking down Sublevel B. Lucy's signal just went dark.**
Maya stopped walking.

She stood in the parking lot, the glass monolith of Aeterna at her back, and felt the temperature of the air change as the sun dropped below the ridgeline. The building's smart windows were already transitioning — rows of electrochromic glass shifting from transparent to opaque in a slow, synchronized wave, floor by floor, like eyelids closing from the top down. Vlad's offices didn't let in the sunset. The upper floors were already dark.

Lucy's signal. The RFID chip implanted — Chloe's best guess — somewhere in or on Lucy's body, pinging a receiver that Chloe had been triangulating for three days. It had been moving in a slow, rhythmic pattern consistent with someone lying in a bed, breathing. Alive. Lucy was alive, or had been.
Now: dark.

Maya's hand was on her phone, the screen still glowing with Chloe's words. Her fingers were cold despite the lingering warmth of the day. She could feel it — something shifting, tectonic and irreversible, like the moment between the gunshot and the wound opening. The podcast had drawn blood. The women's voices had cracked the facade. And Vlad — a thing that cast no shadow, that radiated no heat, that stood in doorways and spoke in threats dressed as welcomes — Vlad was responding.
The war was on. And he had struck first.
Maya ran.

Her shoes hit the asphalt hard, the impact jarring up through her shins. The Civic's door handle was hot under her palm — the sun-baked metal burning like a brand. She wrenched it open, threw herself into the driver's seat, and fumbled the keys into the ignition. The engine caught on the second try.

In the rearview mirror, Aeterna's glass tower stood against the darkening sky, its windows sealed shut, its secrets locked inside. Somewhere beneath it, in a sublevel that didn't exist on any floor plan, Lucy Chen's signal had gone silent.
Maya peeled out of the parking lot, tires screaming, and did not look back again.
